item.title: Israeli Ambassador Michael Leiter on Iran conflict and Rep. Ro Khanna's visit to West Bank
item.description: On "Face the Nation," Israeli Ambassador to the U.S. Michael Leiter said Israel is prepared to restart war with Iran if the U.S. requests it, as the U.S. and Iran trade fire. He also addressed Rep. Ro Khanna, who says he was detained by Israeli settlers in the West Bank.

item.title: Remembering Sen. Lindsey Graham's impact after his sudden death
item.description: Longtime Republican Sen. Lindsey Graham died early Sunday morning after a "brief and sudden illness," his office said. U.S. lawmakers and world leaders are paying tribute to the South Carolina native, and remembering his political and global impact.

item.title: Sen. Tim Scott on Lindsey Graham's death: "America certainly has lost a statesman"
item.description: Sen. Tim Scott remembered fellow South Carolina lawmaker Lindsey Graham on "Face the Nation." He recounted Graham's sense of humor, his history as a "fierce fighter on foreign policy" and his role in "building bridges," including with President Trump.

item.title: Open: This is "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan," July 12, 2026
item.description: This week on "Face the Nation," Sen. Tim Scott and others discuss the death of a giant of the Senate: Lindsey Graham. Also, retired Gen. Frank McKenzie and Israeli Ambassador Michael Leiter look at the latest U.S. and Iranian strikes, and former White House chief of staff Rahm Emanuel addresses the U.S.-Israel relationship.

item.title: Sen. Scott on Lindsey Graham's role in "building bridges," including with Trump
item.description: Republican Sen. Tim Scott of South Carolina remembered Sen. Lindsey Graham as a "powerful leader" following his sudden death​, while emphasizing Graham's role in "building bridges."

item.title: Transcript: Sen. Tim Scott on "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan," July 12, 2026
item.description: The following is the transcript of an interview with Republican Sen. Tim Scott of South Carolina that aired on "Face the Nation with Margaret Brennan" on July 12, 2026.

item.title: This week on "Sunday Morning" (July 12)
item.description: A look at the features for this week's broadcast of the Emmy-winning program, hosted by Jane Pauley.

item.title: Nature: Cape May National Wildlife Refuge
item.description: We leave you this summer Sunday on the seashore at New Jersey's Cape May National Wildlife Refuge. Videographer: Scot Miller.

item.title: Milepost: David Attenborough
item.description: This week, British documentarian Sir David Attenborough, who turned 100 years old in May, broke the record for oldest nominee for a Primetime Emmy Award, earning two nominations this year.

item.title: Grace notes: Art as a reverie of gentle sounds
item.description: French artist and composer Céleste Boursier-Mougenot's "Clinamen," at the Park Avenue Armory in New York City, is a mesmerizing installation in which porcelain bowls floating in giant basins of water collide, producing chiming sounds that reverberate in the 55,000-square-foot hall, to foster a state of grace. Tracy Smith reports.

item.title: Training for a Tuscan tradition: The wine barrel race
item.description: Every August, the medieval Italian town of Montepulciano becomes the backdrop of intense rivalries among neighborhood teams competing in the Bravìo delle Botti – the wine barrel race. Correspondent Seth Doane talks with locals training for the grueling competition: to roll 200-lb. wine barrels uphill through the medieval city's streets.

item.title: U.S. and world leaders pay tribute to Lindsey Graham following sudden death
item.description: The senior U.S. senator for South Carolina died suddenly at 71 on Saturday.

item.title: Behind the scenes of "The Pitt"
item.description: The Emmy-winning HBO Max drama "The Pitt" immerses viewers in the hour-by-hour struggles faced by the overworked-yet-superhuman emergency room staff at the fictional Pittsburgh Trauma Medical Center. CBS News chief medical correspondent Dr. Jon LaPook visits the series' hyper-realistic set at Warner Brothers Studios in Burbank, Calif. (where cast members undergo a two-week medical boot camp), and talks with star, writer, director and executive producer Noah Wylie about why the former "ER" actor returned to the medical drama genre. (The series just received 25 Emmy nominations, including Outstanding Drama Series, the most of any program.) [Originally broadcast Jan. 4, 2026.]

item.title: Sen. Lindsey Graham in 2023 on funding the war in Ukraine: "Great deal for America"
item.description: In 2023, Holly Williams met with Sen. Lindsey Graham and other U.S. lawmakers in Kyiv to discuss America's bankrolling of Ukraine's defense against Russia. Graham called it the best U.S. investment "since we helped Churchill stand up to the Nazis." Graham died Saturday at 71.

item.title: Trump's monumental reimagining of Washington, D.C.
item.description: President Trump has triggered a firestorm of lawsuits over his plans to remake our nation's capital, as opponents fight to preserve it.

item.title: Trump's monumental reimagining of Washington, D.C.
item.description: President Trump has triggered a firestorm of lawsuits over his plans to remake Washington, D.C. – from constructing a 250-foot arch that would dwarf the nearby Lincoln Memorial, to demolishing the East Wing of the White House for a ballroom. CBS News chief White House correspondent Nancy Cordes looks at the president's efforts to reshape our nation's capital, and opponents fighting to block him.

item.title: Typhoon Bavi weakens but still lashes China with strong winds, rain
item.description: Typhoon Bavi weakened Sunday to a tropical storm but was still bringing strong winds and heavy rain to parts of China.

item.title: Passage: In memoriam
item.description: "Sunday Morning" remembers some of the notable figures who left us this week, including "Mary Hartman, Mary Hartman" actress Louise Lasser, and singer Bonnie Tyler, best known for "Total Eclipse of the Heart."

item.title: Gracie Abrams: Now center stage
item.description: At 26, singer-songwriter Gracie Abrams has won praise from critics and fans for her intimate songs – whispered words that become anthems. She talks about her latest album, "Daughter From Hell."

item.title: Extended interview: Gracie Abrams, Aaron Dessner
item.description: In this web exclusive, singer-songwriter Gracie Abrams and producer Aaron Dessner talk with correspondent Tracy Smith about their latest album, "Daughter From Hell."

item.title: Gracie Abrams: Now center stage
item.description: At 26, singer-songwriter Gracie Abrams has won praise from critics and fans for her intimate songs – whispered words that become anthems, and which the shy girl-turned-superstar now performs to sold-out arenas. Correspondent Tracy Smith talks with Abrams about how she worked up the nerve to post her early songs online. Abrams also discusses being the opening act for Olivia Rodrigo and Taylor Swift, and recording her latest album, "Daughter From Hell."

item.title: A child transforms a North Carolina neighborhood
item.description: If you pass by four-year-old Roman Butzlaff's house in Concord, N.C., he will surely greet you with a wave and a "hey." But what began as a friendly gesture became a bond that drew together his neighborhood. Steve Hartman reports on the "family" that Roman built one wave at a time.

item.title: Book excerpt: "They Stole a City" by Lauren Collins
item.description: The New Yorker writer's new book examines how, in 1898, white supremacists staged a coup against Wilmington, N.C.'s multi-racial government – a case study in the sabotage of American democracy.

item.title: The universal nostalgia for "Take Me Home, Country Roads"
item.description: A song about a longing for home, John Denver's first hit in 1971 has been embraced by West Virginia in a big way, and has been appropriated by singers around the world looking for their very own "place I belong."

item.title: Sen. Lindsey Graham dies after "brief and sudden illness"
item.description: President Trump paid tribute to the late senator, who was reportedly scheduled to do an interview on Sunday.

item.title: How white supremacists staged the only successful coup in U.S. history
item.description: In 1898, Wilmington, N.C., was prosperous and integrated. But white supremacists took back control of the city's multi-racial government at gunpoint, and killed scores of Black residents - a little-known story retold in Lauren Collins' "They Stole a City."

item.title: Sen. Lindsey Graham dies after sudden illness | Special Report
item.description: U.S. Senator Lindsey Graham has died after a brief and sudden illness, his office says. Carissa Lawson anchored this CBS News special report.

item.title: At least 2 killed in shooting at Toronto street festival, police say
item.description: At least two people were killed and several more wounded in a shooting Saturday evening at a street festival in Toronto, Canada, authorities said.

item.title: High energy costs from Iran war heighten pressure on struggling farmers
item.description: As the agriculture industry in Louisiana contends with major energy cost hikes brought on by the Iran war, some farmers are unsure if their businesses will survive.

item.title: Man picking up debris loses hand after blast on California beach, family says
item.description: The family says the man was walking along the beach picking up discarded fireworks and other trash when he picked up what they described as a "bomb-like device" that unexpectedly exploded.
